blocks.body: Modals of Deduction help you make a conclusion about a situation now based on facts or evidence.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In this topic you will learn to use must, may, might, could, and can't to make a logical deduction about the present based on what you see or know.","rules":[{"label":"STRONG CONCLUSION","color":"emerald","text":"Must = I am almost sure because there are strong signs.","example":"He must be tired after that run."},{"label":"POSSIBLE EXPLANATION","color":"blue","text":"May / might / could = this is one possible answer, but not certain.","example":"She might be at work."},{"label":"IMPOSSIBLE","color":"amber","text":"Can't = this is logically impossible or almost impossible.","example":"They can't be serious."}]}

{"title":"1. Common patterns","intro":"For present deduction, we often use modal + be or modal + be + -ing.","items":[{"label":"Strong deduction","title":"must be + noun / adjective","subtitle":"must be at home / must be tired"},{"label":"Possible deduction","title":"may / might / could be + noun / adjective","subtitle":"might be busy / could be wrong"},{"label":"Impossible deduction","title":"can't be + noun / adjective","subtitle":"can't be true / can't be him"},{"label":"Action in progress","title":"modal + be + -ing","subtitle":"must be working / might be sleeping"}]}

{"title":"2. How it works","sections":[{"label":"Must = strong conclusion","color":"emerald","description":"Use must when the facts or signs strongly lead to one conclusion.","examples":[{"en":"The lights are off. They must be asleep.","ua":"The lights are off. They must be asleep."},{"en":"She has been studying all day. She must be exhausted.","ua":"She has been studying all day. She must be exhausted."},{"en":"He is answering every question. He must know the topic well.","ua":"He is answering every question. He must know the topic well."}]},{"label":"May / might / could = possible explanation","color":"blue","description":"These modal verbs show that an explanation is possible, but you are not fully sure.","examples":[{"en":"She might be in a meeting now.","ua":"She might be in a meeting now."},{"en":"This key could be the right one.","ua":"This key could be the right one."},{"en":"They may be waiting outside.","ua":"They may be waiting outside."}],"note":"In many contexts, may, might, and could are very close in meaning."},{"label":"Can't = impossible","color":"amber","description":"Use can't when the conclusion goes against the facts or feels logically impossible.","examples":[{"en":"He can't be at home. I just saw him downtown.","ua":"He can't be at home. I just saw him downtown."},{"en":"This can't be the final version. It has too many mistakes.","ua":"This can't be the final version. It has too many mistakes."},{"en":"She can't be sleeping. The TV is still on.","ua":"She can't be sleeping. The TV is still on."}],"note":"Here can't expresses deduction, not prohibition."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Using can for a positive deduction about a specific situation.","wrong":"She can be at home now.","right":"✅ She may be at home now. / She might be at home now.","hint":"For a positive guess about a specific situation, we normally use may / might / could."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using mustn't instead of can't for an impossible conclusion.","wrong":"He mustn't be at home. All the lights are off.","right":"✅ He can't be at home. All the lights are off.","hint":"Mustn't = prohibition, but can't = logical impossibility."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Adding auxiliary do to modal deduction.","wrong":"He doesn't must be tired.","right":"✅ He must be tired.","hint":"After a modal verb, you do not need do / does."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["Must = strong logical conclusion.","May / might / could = possible answer or explanation.","Can't = logically impossible.","For present deduction, we often use modal + be or modal + be + -ing.","Do not mix up can't for deduction with mustn't for prohibition."]}
